title: Guerrilla Auditors: The Politics of Transparency in Neoliberal Paraguay By Kregg Hetherington Duke University Press Books. 2011. 312 pages. $23.95 paper
abstract: The coup d'etat that unseated Paraguay's longstanding dictator Alfredo Stroessner in February of 1989 brought hopes of a different future for the small, landlocked country. However, as Kregg Hetherington's book Guerilla Auditors makes clear, behind the seemingly common hopes of some of the emerging political actors lay very different perspectives. For Hetherington, the democratic reforms following Stroessner's fall situated Paraguayan campesinos in a decidedly awkward position. For the reformers guiding the transition, the campesinos' marginality and poverty evinced the importance of reforms based on transparency and universal (and abstract) equality before the law, but their supposed economic irrationality, illiteracy and populist leanings were considered contrary to the reforms' universal spirit. The reformers have thus excluded the campesinado from the politics of the transition “for its own good.” Paradoxically, campesinos have reacted to this exclusion by openly embracing the transparency project of the reformers, embracing legal documents as “weapons of the weak” in their struggle for land. Embedded in this struggle, however, are more basic assumptions about what documents are and what they do, assumptions that differ considerably between campesinos and reformers. While democratic reformers advocate a liberal notion of property as a result of legal recognition in an abstract code, for campesinos the opposite is true: the material use of the land provides the basis of abstract ownership. Studies of campesino economics often explicitly mention this point, and it is implied in the familiar dualisms of exchange-value and use-value, presence-at-hand and ready-to-hand, etc., that social scientists and philosophers use to talk about objectification.
